critique.summary: The article introduces Cook, a command-line interface designed to orchestrate workflows using AI agents such as Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode. It explains Cook's primitives, loop operators, composition operators, resolvers, and configuration options for managing iterative and parallel AI-driven tasks.
mainPoints: Cook parses three categories of tokens: work (a prompt/agent call), loop operators (for iteration), and composition operators (for parallel execution and result resolution).
Loop operators include repeat (xN), review (which adds a review and gate loop for quality control), and ralph (which manages task-list progression).
Composition operators allow running multiple cooks in parallel isolated environments and combining results using resolvers like pick, merge, and compare.
Cook supports configuration via initialization commands that scaffold project files including agent and model settings, sandbox modes, and logging.
Sandbox modes include agent-level sandboxing (default) and Docker container mode, with OpenCode only supported in Docker mode.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the functionality and features of Cook as a CLI tool for orchestrating AI agent workflows. The technical details about loop operators, composition, and configuration align with typical software design patterns for AI orchestration. However, since this is a project documentation or technical overview hosted on a personal or project site, it does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives. The claims about agent models and sandbox modes are consistent with known AI tooling practices but lack independent verification or third-party validation.
considerations: The article does not provide performance benchmarks or comparisons with other AI orchestration tools.
There is no discussion of limitations, potential biases, or failure modes of using AI agents in this workflow.
Security implications of sandbox modes and token management are not addressed.
The article assumes familiarity with AI models like Claude, Codex, and OpenCode without explaining their capabilities or differences.
No user case studies or real-world applications are provided to demonstrate Cook's effectiveness.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the CLI tool 'cook' for orchestrating Claude Code, with users expressing curiosity about its features, usability, and design. Some appreciate its concise skill installation and interface, while others raise usability and readability concerns.

comments.author: sbinnee
comments.text:
There is a skill installation option. The skill markdown has 180 lines [1].

My take? I like it. It's concise enough for me to try it out. And I love the webpage.

[1] https://github.com/rjcorwin/cook/blob/main/no-code/SKILL.md
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and specific observation about the tool's features and presentation, indicating a genuine evaluation.

comments.author: jemmyw
comments.text: Looks pretty nice. I think a lot of devs have been making similar tools, I've written my own thing that does a work review loop. I like the interface you've made. I'll probably give it a go, but I'm also reluctant to relinquish the control I have when it's my own code doing orchestration.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a comparative perspective and personal experience, adding context to the discussion about similar tools.

comments.author: rc_kas
comments.text: Can someone explain what this is to my n00b brain. I don't get what claude-cli is missing that this adds in?
comments.analysis: This is a genuine request for clarification, reflecting a novice user's perspective but lacks detailed insight.

comments.author: khazhoux
comments.text: How does this handle when Claude needs user input?   To choose an option, grant tool permission, clarify questions…
comments.analysis: Raises a valid usability question about user interaction, which is important for understanding the tool's capabilities.

comments.author: NetOpWibby
comments.text: Dull colors and a display font used for copy makes this website incredibly unpleasant to read.
comments.analysis: Offers subjective but relevant feedback on the website's design and readability, which could impact user experience.

comments.author: vadepaysa
comments.text:
I did a Show HN[0] a few days back with my CLI agent called cook[1] and for a moment I was ecstatic my tool made it to the front page. haha.

[0]: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47262711 [1]: https://getcook.dev
comments.analysis: Personal anecdote expressing excitement; adds context but limited technical insight.

comments.author: nurettin
comments.text:
claude> "We want to add a title section that shows what page we are currently on, use cook to manage the development process"

* coolers whirring, gpus on fire, tokens flying, investors happy, developer goes for 6th break of the day
comments.analysis: Humorous and exaggerated comment likely meant to entertain rather than provide substantive analysis.
